summary: This paper studies distributionally robust reinforcement learning (DR-RL) in a purely online setting under general function approximation. Whereas most existing DR-RL methods assume access to a generative model or a comprehensive offline dataset and are largely confined to tabular problems, the proposed algorithm learns a robust policy directly from online interaction with the nominal environment, optimizing worst-case performance over phi-divergence uncertainty sets. The central algorithmic idea is a dual-based fitted robust Bellman update that jointly estimates the value function and the robust backup operator, wrapped in an optimism/UCB-style confidence construction to drive exploration. On the theoretical side, the authors introduce an intrinsic complexity measure, the robust Bellman-Eluder dimension, and establish what they present as the first sublinear regret guarantee for online DR-RL with general function approximation; the bound is independent of the sizes of the state and action spaces and recovers known sharp rates when specialized to tabular and linear MDPs. The theory is complemented by experiments on a perturbed control task that illustrate the robustness of the learned policy.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
The problem is important and timely: extending distributionally robust RL to the purely online setting with general function approximation removes two of the most restrictive assumptions in the prior literature (a generative model or offline coverage, and a tabular structure), and to my knowledge this is the first work to tackle both simultaneously. The paper is generally well written and well organized, and it does a good job conveying the high-level intuition behind the algorithm; the UCB-based construction is coherent and the reduction of the robust backup to a dual fitted update is elegant. The introduction of the robust Bellman-Eluder dimension is an appealing structural contribution, and characterizing regret through this single quantity, while recovering sharp rates in the tabular and linear regimes, is a clean result. The overall theoretical approach appears sound; although I did not verify every proof line by line, the argument structure is convincing and consistent with the standard optimism-in-the-face-of-uncertainty template.

That said, the originality of the technical machinery is more incremental than the framing suggests. The dual functional techniques underpinning the robust Bellman update draw heavily on prior work (notably Panaganti et al.), and the paper does not clearly delineate what is genuinely new in the algorithm and analysis relative to that line of work; the discussion around this point (for example, the passage from a strictly positive regularizer to the lambda = 0 case) is too terse to let the reader gauge the real technical delta. Relatedly, the motivation for why the online robust setting is fundamentally hard is underdeveloped: fitted learning under general function approximation is standard in non-robust RL, and the paper does not make crisp why it fails to extend directly, nor how the information-bottleneck issue (data from the nominal kernel need not reflect the worst-case kernel) is actually circumvented. The sharpness of the regret bound is also only demonstrated through specialization to tabular and linear MDPs; there is no matching lower bound establishing the robust Bellman-Eluder dimension as the intrinsic complexity measure for the general problem.

On presentation, while the writing is clear at a high level, the paper is not fully self-contained: central objects such as the phi-divergence and the (distributional) Eluder dimension are used without formal definition or intuition, and even a reader familiar with the robust RL literature must reconstruct several concepts from cited sources. There is also a conceptual imprecision in treating robust MDPs and distributionally robust MDPs interchangeably: the development takes a minimum over transition probabilities (as in Iyengar) rather than over distributions of transition kernels (as in Xu and Mannor), and these correspond to different structural assumptions that deserve to be disambiguated. A number of smaller issues (notation inconsistencies in the uncertainty-set definitions, a possible sign in one of the dual expressions, an unexplained baseline OPROVI-TV, and a few missing baselines) further reduce readability.

Finally, the significance of the contribution is at present largely theoretical. Computational aspects are not discussed: it is unclear whether the inner optimization and the confidence-set construction can be solved efficiently, and how the method scales. The empirical evaluation is limited to a single, small discrete-control domain, without larger or continuous benchmarks and without ablations over the key algorithmic parameters, so the experiments do not yet substantiate that the method is practically useful beyond illustrating the concept.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. What are the key new algorithmic and analytical elements relative to the dual functional techniques of Panaganti et al., and what specifically is difficult about moving from a strictly positive regularizer to the lambda = 0 case? More broadly, why does standard fitted learning under general function approximation not extend directly to the robust setting when one interacts only with the nominal environment?
2. In the online setting the collected data is correlated and need not represent the worst-case kernel (the information bottleneck). How is this circumvented, is it via the constraint that all kernels in the uncertainty set remain close to the nominal one, and how do the mixing properties of the data-generating policies enter the regret bound?
3. Can a formal lower bound be established showing that the robust Bellman-Eluder dimension is the fundamental complexity measure for the general problem, rather than sharpness being demonstrated only through specialization to tabular and linear MDPs?
4. What is the computational complexity of the algorithm, and can the inner optimization problem and the confidence-set construction be implemented efficiently and scalably for large-scale or real-time applications?
5. How does the method perform beyond the single discrete domain evaluated, for instance on more complex, high-dimensional, or continuous-action benchmarks, and can the framework and its analysis be extended to continuous control?
limitations: The limitations are not discussed explicitly and should be. In particular, the paper should candidly address the gap between its strong theory and its narrow empirical validation on a single discrete toy domain, the absence of a matching lower bound for the proposed complexity measure, and the open questions around computational tractability and scalability to continuous or high-dimensional settings.
